coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots-based red fluorescence nanoprobe for caspase-1 activity assay and living cell imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Gai-Gai
Lv, Qi-Yan
Song, Xiaojie
Cui, Hui-Fang
coredata.dc:description: The development of sensitive and convenient strategies for monitoring intracellular caspase-1 activity is of great significance. Herein, a novel fluorescence nanoprobe for caspase-1 activity determination was constructed based on surface energy transfer (SET) between red-emissive carbon dots (RCDs) as a donor, and Au nanorods (AuNRs) as an acceptor. The donor/acceptor pair were covalently bridged with a peptide containing a caspase-1 cleavage site (YVAD) at middle. The stability and dispersivity of the AuNR-Pep-RCD nanoprobe was realized/enhanced by pretreating the AuNRs with PEG-SH. Active caspase-1 cleaves the YVAD site, releasing RCDs and recovering the fluorescence. Synergic effect of the red-emission, sensitive and selective cleavage, and excellent SET efficiency contribute to the nanoprobe with high sensitivity and selectivity, especially in biological samples. More importantly, the nanoprobe can be uptaken by living cells, capable of in-situ intracellular caspase-1 activity imaging. The fluorescence recovery is linearly correlated with caspase-1 activity in the range of 5‒300 ng·mL−1 (2.5 × 10−5‒1.3 × 10−3 U·mL−1), with a limit of detection of 1.76 ng·mL−1 (8.8 × 10-6 U·mL−1). In particular, the fluorescence nanoprobe can accurately detect caspase-1 activity in cells extract, and sensitively probe the caspase-1 activation treatment and inhibition treatment for the intracellular caspase-1 activity levels.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Caspase-1 activity
Fluorescence nanoprobe
Red emission
Surface energy transfer
Living cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Caspase-1 activity assay with the AuNR-Pep-RCD nanoprobe
Characterization of the RCDs and the AuNR-Pep-RCD nanoprobe
Energy transfer mechanism between RCDs and AuNRs
Analytical performances of the nanoprobe on caspase-1 activity assay
Applicability on caspase-1 activity assay in cell extract
Fluorescence imaging of caspase-1 in living cells

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC 21345007 ), the Plan for Scientific Innovation Talent of Henan Province to H. F. Cui (Grant number 154200510007 ), and the Natural Science Foundation of Henan Province (Grant number 182300410314 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of the RCDs with HRTEM as inset, and the RCDs size distribution. (B) FT-IR spectra of the RCDs. (C) UV–vis absorption spectrum of the RCDs, and emission spectra of the RCDs under different excitation wavelengths. (D) Fluorescence emission spectra of the RCDs with a series of concentrations. Inset: plot of the corresponding fluorescence peak intensity at 596 nm versus RCDs concentration.

TEM and HRTEM images of (A) AuNRs and (B) AuNR-Pep-RCD nanoprobe. (C) Zeta (ξ) potential of RCDs, AuNRs, AuNR@PEG, AuNR@PEG-Pep, and AuNR-Pep-RCD nanoprobe. (D) UV–vis spectra of AuNRs, AuNR@PEG, AuNR@PEG-Pep, and AuNR-Pep-RCD nanoprobe. (E) Fluorescence emission spectra of RCDs and AuNR-Pep-RCD nanoprobe.

Investigation of the energy transfer mechanism between the RCDs and the AuNRs. (A) Spectral overlap between the emission spectrum of RCDs and the absorption spectrum of AuNRs. (B) FL lifetime decay curves of the RCDs and the AuNR-Pep-RCD nanoprobe (IRF, for instrumental response function).

(A) FL emission spectra of AuNR-Pep-RCD nanoprobe (0.58 nM) in the presence of different concentrations of caspase-1 (0–300 ng/mL−1). Inset: plot of FL intensity increment (F1-F0) versus caspase-1 concentration. F0 and F1 represent the fluorescence intensity of the nanoprobe before and after the addition of caspase-1 sample, respectively. (B) Specificity of the AuNRs-Pep-RCD nanoprobe towards caspase-1 (100 ng/mL), in comparisons with other interferents. Data represent mean values ± standard deviation (S.D.), n = 3.

Confocal fluorescence images of MCF-7 and RAW264.7 cells after incubation with AuNR-Pep-RCD nanoprobe for 4 h. The red color is the fluorescence from the nanoprobe in response to active Caspase-1, and the blue color results from Hoechst 33342 for cellular nuclei staining. Scale bar: 50 μm.
Schematic illustration of the preparation process and the caspase-1 activity response mechanism of the AuNR-Pep-RCD nanoprobe.
Caspase-1 activity assay results in RAW264.7 cell extract with the AuNR-Pep-RCD nanoprobe.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots-based red fluorescence nanoprobe for caspase-1 activity assay and living cell imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life Sciences, Zhengzhou University, Kexue Road No. 100, Zhengzhou 450001, People’s Republic of China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Life Sciences, Zhengzhou University, Kexue Road No. 100, Zhengzhou 450001, People’s Republic of China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel fluorescent “turn on” nanoprobe for caspase-1 activity assay has been constructed based on conjugation of AuNRs and RCDs through a short peptide. This fabrication and detection strategy provides high sensitivity, specificity, and stability in cell extract sample detection and in-situ living cell imaging.

The development of sensitive and convenient strategies for monitoring intracellular caspase-1 activity is of great significance. Herein, a novel fluorescence nanoprobe for caspase-1 activity determination was constructed based on surface energy transfer (SET) between red-emissive carbon dots (RCDs) as a donor, and Au nanorods (AuNRs) as an acceptor. The donor/acceptor pair were covalently bridged with a peptide containing a caspase-1 cleavage site (YVAD) at middle. The stability and dispersivity of the AuNR-Pep-RCD nanoprobe was realized/enhanced by pretreating the AuNRs with PEG-SH. Active caspase-1 cleaves the YVAD site, releasing RCDs and recovering the fluorescence. Synergic effect of the red-emission, sensitive and selective cleavage, and excellent SET efficiency contribute to the nanoprobe with high sensitivity and selectivity, especially in biological samples. More importantly, the nanoprobe can be uptaken by living cells, capable of in-situ intracellular caspase-1 activity imaging. The fluorescence recovery is linearly correlated with caspase-1 activity in the range of 5‒300 ng·mL−1 (2.5 × 10−5‒1.3 × 10−3 U·mL−1), with a limit of detection of 1.76 ng·mL−1 (8.8 × 10-6 U·mL−1). In particular, the fluorescence nanoprobe can accurately detect caspase-1 activity in cells extract, and sensitively probe the caspase-1 activation treatment and inhibition treatment for the intracellular caspase-1 activity levels.

ce:section.ce:para: Caspase-1, also known as an interleukin-1β converting enzyme (ICE), is a cysteine endopeptidase of the caspase family [1]. Caspase-1 acts as the key proinflammatory caspase, participating in the initiation and execution of inflammation and pyroptosis process [2,3]. The main substrates of caspase-1 are two pro-inflammatory cytokines, pro-interleukin-1β (pro-IL-1β) and pro-interleukin-18 (pro-IL-18), which are expressed in various kinds of cells. Many inflammatory diseases are related to the dysregulation of caspase-1 activity, including rheumatoid arthritis, inflammatory bowel disease, and septic shock [4]. The inflammasome assists caspase-1 oligomerization, leading to the activation of caspase-1 from inactive zymogen [5]. The specific activation process of caspase-1 makes it hard to define the proteolytic activity only through quantification of its physical abundance, which is normally achieved via antibodies or proteomics strategies. Therefore, intracellular caspase-1 activity detection that can offer ideal sensitivity, specificity, and real-time monitoring capability, show massive potentials in revelation of the molecular mechanisms of relative biological processes and exploitation of novel targeted drugs.

The development of fluorescence probes shows exciting applications in the analytical field, such as biosensing, imaging, and photodevice. Some intriguing fluorescence probes have been established for caspase-1 activity sensings, such as organic fluorescence probes [6–8], bioluminescence sensors [9–12], quantum-dot-peptide conjugates [13,14], and aggregation-induced emission probe [15]. These fluorimetries provide high sensitivity, specificity and wide linear range in caspase-1 activity detection. However, the low biocompatibility, high photobleaching (like organic fluorophores) of these methods is a major stumbling block for further application in biological research and drug discovery.

Carbon dots (CDs) possess several attractive characteristics, including excellent optical properties, high physicochemical stability, feasible surface modification, good biocompatibility, and low-cost preparation [16]. Therefore, carbon dots-based analytical strategies have been widely used in biosensing [17], bioimaging [18,19], theranostic [20,21], optoelectronic devices [22,23], targeted drug delivery [24,25], etc. However, most of the reported carbon dots emit light in blue to green region under excitation of ultraviolet (UV) to blue-light, leading to high interference by auto-fluorescence of the biological matrix and decreased sensitivity [26]. Therefore, the usage of red emissive carbon dots (RCDs) for nanoprobe fabrication could enhance their tissue penetrability and achieve high imaging sensitivity in situ [27]. Recently, some inspiring studies have reported the controlled solvothermal methods for full-colour CDs preparation based on the bottom-up route. For example, a series of CDs with tunable emission and high quantum yield (QY) were synthesized from l-glutamic acid and o-phenylenediamine via solvothermal reactions [28]. Full-emissive CDs were synthesized from two small precursors (citric acid and urea) through the employment of different solvents [29]. These reports provide extensive inspirations in CDs synthesis. Nevertheless, the application of RCDs in bioprobe construction is a big challenge owing to the complex physiological environment, which can affect their optical property and stability.

In this study, RCDs were utilized to construct a “turn on” fluorescence nanoprobe for highly sensitive and quantitative analysis of caspase-1 activity based on the surface energy transfer (SET) mechanism between RCDs (donors) and Au nanorods (AuNRs, acceptors) (Scheme 1 ). The AuNRs were employed as both quenchers and nanocarriers for their tunable strong absorption spectra and large specific surface area. The longitudinal absorption band of AuNRs was tuned to largely overlap the emission band of RCDs by adjusting their aspect ratios. More importantly, the surface of Au nanoparticles (including AuNRs) allows a 22 nm detection length scale following the SET mechanism, instead of the limitation of a 10 nm detection length in the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism [30–32]. A short peptide containing a caspase-1 specific substrate (YVAD) served as a bridge connecting the RCDs and AuNRs [14]. The nanoprobe can be activated in the appearance of active caspase-1, restoring fluorescence signal. In this approach, multiple donors can be conjugated on one AuNR, thus decreasing the number of nanoprobes necessary for detection. Moreover, to reduce nonspecific adsorption of RCDs on AuNRs in nanoprobe fabrication and solve the instability problem of nanoprobes in complex bio-matrix, the AuNRs were coated with electroneutral PEG molecular via ligand-change reaction at first. The RCDs and the AuNR-Pep-RCD nanoprobe were synthesized and characterized, the energy transfer mechanism between the RCDs and AuNRs was investigated, and the analytical performances of the nanoprobes-based caspase-1 assay in cell extract and living cells were evaluated. The AuNR-Pep-RCD nanoprobe offers a highly sensitive and selective caspase-1 activity measurement and effective intracellular optical imaging, showing great potentials for biological and medical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Caspase-1 activity assay with the AuNR-Pep-RCD nanoprobe
ce:section.ce:para: The caspase-1 activity assay was initiated by adding 5 μL caspase-1 sample solution into 0.58 nM 500 μL AuNR-Pep-RCD nanoprobe solution (in reaction buffer). The obtained mixture was incubated for 2 h at 37 °C, and then the fluorescence spectra were measured to monitor and determine the caspase-1 activity. The fluorescence intensity increment (F1-F0) was taken as the caspase-1 activity assay signal, where F1 and F0 represent the fluorescence intensity of the nanoprobe after and before the addition of caspase-1 sample, respectively. Standard caspase-1 samples were used to evaluate the sensitivity and linearity, and cell extract was detected to assess the applicability for biological samples. The caspase-1 activity in cell extract was determined by putting the assay signal value into the calibration curve obtained from the standard caspase-1 samples. In addition, the applicability and the accuracy of the nanoprobe was also evaluated by determining the recovery rate of caspase-1 activity in the standard caspase-1 spiked cell extract.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the RCDs and the AuNR-Pep-RCD nanoprobe
ce:section.ce:para: The RCDs were synthesized via a solvothermal method using citric acid and urea as carbon sources according to the previous report [33]. The average diameter of the RCDs is 2.57 nm (Fig. 1 A), with a 0.21 nm uniform lattice fringe of d-spacing for [1100] lattice plane of the graphene (inset of Fig. 1A). In the FT-IR spectrum of the RCDs (Fig. 1B), the very broad absorption peak at 2000−3300 cm−1 is assigned to υ(OH) [34]. The characteristic absorption peaks of υ(CO)/(CN) or (CN) appear at 1450-1770 cm−1, which are associated with carboxyl, and CN aromatic heterocycles [35]. The absorption band peaked at 1551 cm−1 is attributed to υas(COO) in carboxylate groups [34]. The FT-IR spectra suggest abundant carboxyl groups on the RCDs surface. The XRD pattern (Supplementary Fig. S1) contains a broad peak centered at a diffraction angle of 27.5°, corresponding to the ordered layer stacking in the carbon core. The full XPS spectra shown in Supplementary Fig. S2A suggest three typical peaks, including C 1s (285 eV), N 1s (400 eV), and O 1s (532 eV) [29]. The relative atomic ratio of oxygen to carbon (O/C) is 30.63 %. In high-resolution XPS spectra, the C 1s band (Fig. S2B) contains three peaks, corresponding to CC/CC at 284.8 eV (sp2 carbons), CO at 287.4 eV (carbonyl carbons), and COOH at 290.2 eV (carboxyl carbons) [35]. The N 1s band (Fig. S2C) can be deconvoluted into three peaks at 399.6 eV, 400.6 eV, and 401 eV for pyrrolic N, graphitic N, and amino N, respectively [33]. The O 1s band (Fig. S2D) can be deconvoluted into three peaks at 531.3 eV, 532.7 eV, and 533.6 eV, representing CO, COH/CO, and OCO, respectively [33]. The TEM, FT-IR, XRD, and XPS data indicate that the as-prepared RCDs consist of π-conjugated domains in the carbon core and a relatively high-oxidation surface with amorphous regions [35]. The zeta (ξ) potential of the RCDs is determined to be −43 mV (Fig. 2 C), further proving the abundance of carboxyl groups on the RCDs surface.

The UV–vis absorbance spectrum of the RCDs (Fig. 1C) displays a major absorption band at 538 nm, corresponding to the extensive sp2-conjugated domain [36]. The excitation-wavelength-independent photoluminescence occurs in the orange-red region (λmax = 596 nm) (Fig. 1C). The fluorescence intensity is closely related to the concentrations of RCDs. As shown in Fig. 1D, the fluorescence intensity increases first with the increase of the RCDs concentration, showing a linear range at 0‒0.01 mg·mL−1 RCDs (Fig. 1D, inset). Further increased RCDs concentration (>0.05 mg·mL−1) results in a decrease of luminous intensity. At high concentration, RCDs solution exhibit weaker photoluminescence because of aggregation-induced luminescence self-quenching causing by the Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) between RCD particles themselves [37]. Since the absorbance spectra of RCDs partially overlap their emission spectra (shown in Fig. 1C). This characteristic benefits quantifying the RCDs in a dilute solution. Furthermore, the time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) spectra of the RCDs were measured, indicating a double-exponential lifetime of 3.470 ns (Supplementary Table 1S). These results indicate that the molecular state and surface state synergistically contribute to photoluminescence [38].

The seed-mediated growth method was employed for AuNRs synthesis with the help of the cationic surfactant CTAB [39]. As shown in Fig. 2A, the AuNRs show a typical uniform rod structure with an average length of 47 nm and an average width of 20.9 nm, with an aspect ratio of 2.2. The high-resolution TEM image of the AuNRs (inset of Fig. 2A) shows clear lattice fringes of 0.2 nm interplanar spacing. In contrast, the AuNR-Pep-RCD nanoprobe possesses core/satellite-like morphology, as shown in Fig. 2B. Some small dot-like particles (pointed out with yellow arrows and circle lines) scatter around the nanorods. The distance between the nanodots and the rods is less than 15 nm (the HRTEM image on the right of Fig. 2B), directly confirming the successful fabrication of the nanoprobe.

The assembly processes in the AuNR-Pep-RCD nanoprobe fabrication were characterized via ξ potential analysis, UV–vis spectroscopy, and fluorescence spectroscopy. The pristine AuNRs are coated with a dense CTAB bilayer, expressing abundant positive charges with a ξ potential of +38 mV (Fig. 2C). In our initial attempt in the nanoprobe fabrication, it was found that the AuNRs very easily aggregate during the short peptide immobilization, and hardly evitable aggregate in the RCDs connection. To solve this problem, a ligand-exchange reaction with PEG-SH as a ligand was introduced. After modified with PEG-SH via Au-S bond, the ξ potential of the AuNR@PEG become +8 mV. Then, the ξ potential of AuNR@PEG-Pep gets to −33 mV through the second step ligand-exchange reaction with the caspase-1 specific peptide as a ligand. The second step ligand exchange reaction could be facilitated by the high concentration of peptide-SH under the irradiation of ultrasonic. The short peptide contains a terminal –SH to facilitate the formation of Au-S bond at the AuNRs surface, and –NH2 at its other end for coupling RCDs. More carboxyl groups on the short peptide provide it with a negative net-charge. After coupled with the RCDs, the ξ potential of the nanoprobe expresses at −15 mV, attributed to the carboxyl group consumption of the RCDs. The UV–vis spectrum of AuNRs exhibits two typical peaks at 516 nm and 658 nm (Fig. 1D), representing two surface plasmon resonance (SPR) bands, as the oscillation of the conduction band electrons of the rods in width (transverse SPR) and length (longitudinal LSPR), respectively [40]. The surface modifications blue-shifts the LSPR peak slightly to 650 nm (Fig. 1D). Conjugation of RCDs to AuNR@PEG-Pep leads to significant luminescence quenching of the RCDs (Fig. 2E). The AuNR-Pep-RCD nanoprobe solution is very stable, without precipitation during several months of storage at 4 °C. The introduction of the PEG-SH ligand-exchange step can not only stabilize the nanoprobe during fabrication, and also enhance the nanoprobe stability during storage. The high stability should provide the nanoprobe with good application property in complex bio-matrix.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Energy transfer mechanism between RCDs and AuNRs
ce:section.ce:para: The energy transfer mechanism of the luminescence quenching between RCDs and AuNRs in the AuNR-Pep-RCD nanoprobe was investigated. Fig. 3 A displays the overlap of the fluorescence emission band of RCDs and the LSPR absorption band of AuNRs. The large overlap between the two bands can provide high efficiency of energy transfer between the donor and the acceptor. The fluorescence time-resolved decay curves of the RCDs and AuNR-Pep-RCD nanoprobe are shown in Fig. 3B, with instrumental response function (IRF). Accordingly, the fluorescence (FL) lifetime of the AuNR-Pep-RCD nanoprobe was compared with that of the RCDs. After conjugation of AuNRs, the FL lifetime of RCDs obviously decreases from 3.470 ns to 2.147 ns (Supplementary Table S1), implying the existence of SET [41] and the impossibility of static quenching [31] between the RCDs and AuNRs in the nanoprobe. In contrast, the introduction of AuNRs to the RCDs solution without covalent coupling follows a static quenching mechanism, for which the Stern-Volmer plot exhibits an upward curvature [42] (Supplementary Fig. S3). Based on theoretical confirmation, in this nanoprobe system the energy transfer process between RCDs and AuNRs fits well with the SET theory. (as shown in Supplementary Information). Therefore, a SET type fluorescence nanoprobe from the RCDs donor and the AuNRs acceptor was fabricated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performances of the nanoprobe on caspase-1 activity assay
ce:section.ce:para: Before evaluating the analytical performances of the nanoprobe, some conditions in nanoprobe fabrication and caspase-1 activity assay were optimized by comparing the F1-F0 assay signal values. The ratio of the AuNRs to the short peptide was optimized as 1:1000 molar ratio (Fig. S4A). The optimum sample incubation time of the nanoprobe was determined to be 2 h (Fig. S4B). Caspase-1 cleaves the specific section (YVAD) of the short peptide bridge in the nanoprobe, releasing the RCDs from the AuNRs and emitting luminescence. The cleavage proceeds gradually and may complete at about 2 h. In the evolution of the analytical performances, we employ the unit of ng·mL−1 caspase-1 as the caspase-1 activity for simplicity. For clarity, the real activity of the recombinant human caspase-1 protein used in the experiment is noted here to be about 5000 Unit/mg−1. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the fluorescence intensity of the AuNR-Pep-RCD nanoprobe increased with the increase of caspase-1 concentration. The inset of Fig. 4A shows the proportional relationship between the concentration of caspase-1 and the assay signal (i.e., F1-F0). The linear response ranges from 5 ng·mL−1 to 300 ng·mL−1 caspase-1, following the equation of F1-F0 = 15.9838 + 218.9206lnCcaspase-1, with R2 = 0.9724. The detection limit is 1.76 ng·mL−1 (calculated under 3σ rule, and the S.D. value of blanks samples was taken as the noise). The sensitivity of this nanoprobe is comparable to or better than those previously reported caspase-1 activity assay methods, as shown in Supplementary Table 2S. Compared with the reported fluorescence caspase-1 probes, which always using organic fluorophores, our nanoprobe involved two inorganic nanoparticles, showing better photostability, biocompatibility, and economy. In addition, the red-emission florescence of this nanoprobe could effectively reduce the autofluorescence-induced false-positive results. The novel nanoprobe can act as a sensitive, fast, and simple fluorescence probe for caspase-1 activity assay.

Specificity is another vital property to identify the target within the complex biological environment. As shown in Fig. 4B, the AuNR-Pep-RCD nanoprobe exhibits a significantly high fluorescence response towards caspase-1. In contrast, the inorganic ions (Na+, K+, Cl−), other enzymes (GOx, GLU, Trypsin), and protein (HSA) don’t trigger remarkable fluorescence recovery.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applicability on caspase-1 activity assay in cell extract
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the applicability of this nanoprobe on caspase-1 activity assay in complex biological matrices, the RAW264.7 cell extract was collected and detected. As shown in Table 1 , the endogenous caspase-1 activity level in the RAW264.7 cell extract was determined to be 2.07 ng·mL−1. It has been reported that caspase-1 activity in U937 cell extract was 4.93 ng·mL−1 (detected through chemiluminescence) [43]. The concentration of caspase-1 was about 6.4 ng·mL−1 in THP-1 and U937 cells extract, as detected by using a bioluminescence activity-based probe [9]. The detected caspase-1 activity value in the RAW264.7 cell extract by using the nanoprobe in this work is in the same order of magnitude with the reported values in the other cell lines.

The detection accuracy and applicability of the AuNR-Pep-RCD nanoprobe in biological samples was also evaluated by determining the recovery rate of caspase-1 activity in standard caspase-1 spiked cell extract samples (Table 1). The results manifest an excellent accuracy, according to the 94.0 %‒100.1 % recovery rate in various concentrations of caspase-1 spiked samples. The retentivity of the nanoprobe-based assay in biological sample detection is very good, indicated by the low RSD (relative standard deviation) value (≤4.0 %).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence imaging of caspase-1 in living cells
ce:section.ce:para: Before living cell imaging, the cytotoxicity of the AuNR-Pep-RCD nanoprobe was evaluated via cell viability measurement. As shown in Supplementary Fig. S5, the cell viabilities are over 85 % after incubated with various concentrations of the nanoprobe for 4 h. These data demonstrate the high biocompatibility of the AuNR-Pep-RCD nanoprobe.

After confirming the applicability of the nanoprobe for caspase-1 activity assay in cell extract, the ability of this nanoprobe to enter living cells and monitor intracellular caspase-1 activity levels in-situ was tested using RAW264.7 cells and MCF-7 cells as models. The cells were incubated with 0.5 nM nanoprobe for 2 h, then treated with or without LPS, which is a well-known stimulator for NOD-like receptor (NLRP) inflammasomes formation and activating caspase-1 [44,45]. The living cell images were recorded with confocal fluorescence microscopy. As anticipated, only very slight or negligible red fluorescence signals are observed in the cells after nanoprobe loading, because of very low caspase-1 levels within resting cells. After LPS stimulation for 2 h, obviously increased red fluorescence signals are observed, indicating the nanoprobe can sensitively and in-situ response to the LPS stimulated caspase-1 activation in living cells. The AuNR-Pep-RCD nanoprobe can enter living cells, confirming the AuNRs act as a carrier for the short peptide and RCDs delivery. As shown in the merged image, the red fluorescence signals are scattered within the cytosol nearby the blue signal-labeled nucleus. This is because the enzymatic process of cytokines precursors by caspase-1 major occurs in the cytosol or the secretory lysosome [46]. To further investigate the sensitivity and the applicability of the nanoprobe for living cell caspase-1 activity monitoring, the pan-caspase inhibitor Z-VAD-FMK was used together with LPS for cell treatment. The introduction of Z-VAD-FMK leads to a remarkable fluorescence weakening (Fig. 5 , lower line). These results prove that the AuNR-Pep-RCD is a sensitive and specific fluorescence nanoprobe for in-situ monitoring and imaging caspase-1 activity level in living cells.

A novel fluorescent “turn on” nanoprobe for caspase-1 activity assay has been constructed based on conjugation of AuNRs and RCDs through a short peptide. The LSPR absorption band of AuNRs has been tuned to largely overlap with the RCDs emission band, resulting in an efficient SET between the RCDs donor and the AuNRs acceptor in the nanoprobe. The introduction of PEG-SH into the fabrication steps solved the problem of AuNRs aggregation and provides the nanoprobe with high stability in complex bio-matrices. Furthermore, the AuNR-Pep-RCD nanoprobe is non-toxic to cells. More importantly, the red emission, specific peptide cleavage, nanosize, and high stability synergistically contribute to the nanoprobe's high sensitivity, specificity, and stability in cell extract sample detection and in-situ living cell imaging. This fluorescence nanoprobe might be a powerful tool for the revelation of the molecular mechanisms of caspase-1 related biological processes and the screening of novel drugs. The fabrication and detection strategy could provide a platform and guidance for other protease activity nanoprobe design, fabrication, and sensing.

Gai-Gai Wang: Investigation, Conceptualization, Methodology. Qi-Yan Lv: Investigation, Data curation, Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Xiaojie Song: Visualization, Formal analysis. Hui-Fang Cui: Conceptualization, Methodology, Project administration, Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no competing financial interests or personal relationships in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC 21345007), the Plan for Scientific Innovation Talent of Henan Province to H. F. Cui (Grant number 154200510007), and the Natural Science Foundation of Henan Province (Grant number 182300410314).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2021.130285.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Human ICE/CED-3 protease nomenclature
Cell

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E.S. Alnemri, D.J. Livingston, D.W. Nicholson, et al., Human ICE/CED-3 Protease Nomenclature, Cell 87 (1996) 171. http://doi.org/10.1016/S0092-8674(00)81334-3.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Activation of interleukin- 1β by a co-induced protease
FEBS Lett.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The gasdermins, a protein family executing cell death and inflammation
Nat. Rev. Immunol.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Broz, P. Pelegrín, F. Shao, The gasdermins, a protein family executing cell death and inflammation, Nat. Rev. Immunol. 20 (2020) 143-157. http://doi.org/10.1038/s41577-019-0228-2.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Miss Gai-Gai Wang graduated and got her master degree in School of Life Science, Zhengzhou University, China in August 2020. Her research area is nanotechnology and bioanalysis.

Dr. Qi-Yan Lv has been a lecture in School of Life Sciences, Zhengzhou University, China since 2017. She received her Doctorate of Science from Research Center for Eco-Environmental Sciences, Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2016. Her research interests are nanotechnology and bioanalysis.

Prof. Hui-Fang CUI has been a full professor in School of Life Sciences, Zhengzhou University, China since 2007. She got her Ph.D degree from Department of Biological Sciences, National University of Singapore in 2006, and worked as a postdoctoral research fellow at Department of Biological Sciences, National University of Singapore from 2007 to 2008, and at School of Chemical and Biomedical Engineering, Nanyang Technological University, Singapore from 2006 to 2007. Her research interests are biosensors, biotechnology, and nanotechnology.
